title: Versal capital letterforms
subtitle: Building up compound-stroke pen capitals for drop caps and chapter headings
excerpt: Versals are the large decorated capital letters used at the opening of a manuscript page or chapter. Unlike the broad-edge capitals of the Foundational or Roman hand, a versal is built up from multiple strokes with the pen nearly empty, thickening the stems and bowls by applying overlapping thin strokes. The result is a capital that scales to any size.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on built-up capitals and versals. Internet Archive scan available. Johnston documented the compound-stroke versal construction and the relationship between pen angle and stroke build-up.

glossaryTerms.definition: A large built-up capital letter used at the opening of a manuscript chapter or decorated page, constructed from multiple overlapping strokes rather than a single broad-edge pass.
The main vertical or near-vertical stroke of a capital letter, such as the upright of a B or the verticals of an H.
A very thin terminal stroke at the top and bottom of a stem, added with the pen corner at the end of the main stroke.

content.text: A
versal

is not made in a single sweep like a Foundational capital. It is built in stages: the outline strokes first, then the fill, then the serifs. The pen is held almost empty of ink for the outline strokes, which produces fine hairline edges that define the letter's profile before the body is filled in. Johnston described this method as the basis of all large-scale script work.
What you need
Nib: a broad-edge nib in size 0 (the broadest Mitchell Roundhand) for a capital at approximately 30 mm height. Use a smaller nib for smaller letters.
Paper: cartridge, 130 gsm. If the paper is very absorbent, dust lightly with powdered gum sandarac before writing to resist ink bleed.
Ink: sumi ink at writing consistency. Versals require a slightly thicker ink than running-hand work to prevent bleeding into the filled stem.
Nib angle and pen hold
Hold the nib at approximately 30 degrees from the baseline for the
stem

strokes. The pen rests between the index and middle fingers with the thumb supporting from the side; the hand rests on the side of the little finger or a guard sheet. Keep the nib nearly empty of ink when making the outline edges of the letter: dip, then wipe the nib on the edge of the ink bottle so only the very tip retains ink.
Building a versal O
Stroke 1: draw the left edge of the O in a single curved stroke from 11 o'clock to 5 o'clock. The stroke is a hairline edge, not a filled curve.
Stroke 2: draw the right edge of the O as a mirror curve from 1 o'clock to 7 o'clock.
Fill: load the nib more generously and fill the space between the two curves with smooth, continuous strokes working from top to bottom.
Add the top thin arc (the thinnest part of the O where the curves meet at 12 o'clock and 6 o'clock). This is worked with the pen corner, not the full nib width.
Allow to dry fully before working adjacent letters. Wet ink at versal scale will blur if the paper is moved before it sets.
Adding hairline serifs
For straight-sided letters (I, H, N, M), add
hairline serifs

at head and tail of each stem. Hold the nib nearly empty and apply a tiny horizontal flick at 90 degrees to the stem, then draw the flick into the stem with a curved return. Two strokes: the bracket curves into the stem end.
Common mistakes

attrs.cause: The outline strokes were too thin and did not form a clear wall; ink flowed past the edges during filling.

content.text: Use slightly thicker ink for the outline strokes, or allow the outline strokes to fully dry before filling. The dried outline acts as a barrier.

attrs.symptom: Stem is wider on one side than the other
attrs.cause: The two edge strokes were not drawn symmetrically around the intended centre line.

content.text: Rule a very light pencil centre line before drawing the outline strokes, and confirm the edges are placed symmetrically before filling.
Where it leads

Versals can be embellished with penwork flourishes, colour fills, or gilding on raised gesso. The plain versals described here are the first stage before those applied arts. They are also the correct capital to use at the opening of a hand-bound journal or a calligraphy quote panel.
